comment_text: The funny thing that people in Iran seem to totally miss is that oil prices going up and losing capacity in the Persian Gulf while bad for the US and many of its allies, is not really that bad for Trump. He has strongly supported domestic oil production. Higher prices makes that look much better and gives extra profits to people highly likely to donate some of the windfall to republicans. Also while oil has global market prices, you cannot really move it around freely to get the best price. Countries that imported oil from the Gulf cannot just replace it with the oil going to the US or Europe even if they offer a higher price. Logistics just isn't there. Not to mention that if the US and the EU decide that the oil they used to buy must still go to them not to Asia that is what will happen. Nobody is going to piss off major powers with real military power they have decades long relationship with so that they can get higher profit for a short time. Oil producers will always choose higher price selling to their existing customers over making people who can home deliver heavily armed men angry. comment_text: 6:40 I think you're asking the wrong question. What has Iran to gain if it continues attacking neighboring countries when the US retreats? What has Iran to gain from keeping the strait of Hormuz closed after a one sided retreat of the US? I have a hard time coming up with a decent answer to those questions. Now, though maybe illegal according to international law, they have a reasonable explanation for why they do so. They are under existential threat. After a one-sided retreat from the US that excuse is gone and any leniency the world has for these actions now will quickly evaporate. Iran would be far better off leaving its neighbors alone and in the process signaling 'sorry, we had no choice , but now the existential risk is gone we leave you alone'. If instead of attacking US assets in neighboring countries Iran would focus on Israel their actions would have firm grounding in international law, they have more assets to degrade Israel and they may actually gain in standing with their neighbors. After all, most arab countries hate Israel and with Israel leadership more and more open to the more extreme versions of the greater Israel project and talking about who are the next threats to Israeli hegemony in the region middle eastern countries see Israel as a very clear danger for the region. IMHO the best Iran can do on a one sided retreat of the US is to stop all hostilities other than those directly aimed at Israel. It is not only best for themselves, it is signaling very clearly to the world that the US abandons their closest ally if it suits them. Effectively throwing Israel under the bus is much more likely to land Trump in hot water at home than throwing a few rockets on their basis in the middle east. Those bases were low level targeted by all kinds of groups already long before the current war with basically nobody talking about it. Nobody really cares about that.

comment_text: Iran has concentrated almost entirely on offensive operations, with virtually no defensive measures in place. Its system has been automated and decentralized, giving it a temporary edge. Yet this advantage will not endure. Israel and the United States are systematically covering the vast expanse of Iran—a nation of mountains and countless underground facilities—and completing round after round of strikes. For nearly five decades, Iran has financed and armed its allied terrorist groups—Hamas, Hezbollah, the Houthis, and others. Today, however, those groups have been reduced to shadows of their former strength. Iran has even betrayed its own financiers, such as Qatar and Turkey, undermining their trust and exposing its duplicity. This pattern of deception underscores the regime’s destructive and malevolent nature. The reality is clear: Iran’s strategy, built on aggression, manipulation, and betrayal, cannot be sustained indefinitely. Israel and the United States, with their declared objectives and advanced capabilities, are steadily dismantling the networks Iran has cultivated. What remains is the stark evidence of Iran’s willingness to sacrifice allies, destabilize regions, and prioritize destruction over the welfare of its own people. In contrast, the United States and Israel have launched nearly 20,000 sorties and heavy strikes against Iran, while simultaneously defending their own territories with advanced technology. Of course, no system is entirely foolproof—some attacks inevitably penetrate their defenses. Israel has adopted a dual strategy: striking Iran while intercepting its incoming attacks, successfully neutralizing about 90% of them. The GCC, meanwhile, has concentrated primarily on interception, achieving similar levels of success. The objectives of Israel and the US are clear and pre-declared: the destruction of nuclear weapons and missile capabilities. For 47 years, Iran has openly proclaimed—first in 1976 and again in 2015—that its singular aim is the eradication of Israel and the United States, targeting Jews and Christians alike. Rather than prioritizing the welfare of its own citizens, Iran has invested in tunnels and alliances with Russia, China, North Korea, Qatar, Hamas, Hezbollah, and others to pursue this destructive mission. Dismantling such a network will take time, but Israel and the US will find relief once it is accomplished—finally able to rest with greater security. Only now has the world begun to grasp the scale of Iran’s amassed power. Even after more than two years of sustained strikes against Hamas and Hezbollah, remnants of these groups remain. Had they not been exposed, the situation could have been far worse. One truth remains constant: in the end, God will triumph over evil. That is the outcome toward which events are moving, though hardship will accompany the process. Israel will inevitably suffer some blows, for no defense system can guarantee absolute protection. The scale of the attacks is staggering—200 to 400 at a time—yet fewer than ten typically land. Cruise and ballistic missiles, however, present greater challenges to interception. Iran, for its part, has not intercepted even a single strike. In time, Iran’s offensive will falter, and Israel and the US will achieve their objectives. Those who undermine sovereignty, finance their schemes through drugs, counterfeit gold, extortion, and deceit—these agents of chaos—seek only domination. What contribution has this faction made to the world beyond unrest? What have they offered their own citizens, other than death and oppression? Do they enjoy freedom? The IRGC manipulates religion to maintain control, while families cry out in protest on the streets. Ordinary people are not part of these orchestrated demonstrations.

comment_text: Disrupting electrical power and related infrastructure in Iran raises serious legal and humanitarian concerns, as it risks causing disproportionate harm to the innocent and brave Iranian people. Such actions may adversely affect access to essential services, undermine basic living conditions, and impede individuals’ ability to communicate with one another. In circumstances where internet access has been completely shut down by the authorities, satellite-based channels may constitute one of the few remaining means through which people can access information and maintain contact with the outside world; interruptions to electricity further compound this deprivation. From a legal and human rights perspective, measures that broadly impact civilian infrastructure may undermine the protection of fundamental rights, including access to information, freedom of expression, and the right to peaceful assembly. Prolonged power outages can therefore restrict the capacity of the innocent and brave Iranian people to organize, communicate, and participate in lawful civic activities, including peaceful demonstrations.
